title: Day 6 · Session + Memory
subtitle: This map focuses on Day 6's three memory boundaries: session JSONL continuation, layered system prompt injection, and memdir long-term memory across sessions.

diagrams.label: Session ledger
diagrams.title: How Session JSONL lets the Agent continue
diagrams.summary: The CLI uses --resume / -c to create or load a Session; run_agent restores messages from Session.history and appends each new turn back to disk.

nodes.label: CLI Runtime
nodes.detail: The CLI parses prompt, cwd, and session flags. No flag creates a new session; -c loads the latest; --resume loads a specific JSONL by id.

nodes.label: Session
nodes.detail: Session wraps session_id, file_path, history, and append_messages. The Agent Loop does not care about the directory layout.

nodes.label: JSONL ledger
nodes.detail: One message per JSON row. Append-only storage keeps the implementation simple: a crash can lose the current turn, but never rewrites the whole history.

nodes.label: messages
nodes.detail: When resuming, Session.history becomes the messages list first. The current user prompt is then appended to memory and persisted to JSONL.

nodes.label: Provider
nodes.detail: The model still reads messages and returns tool_use or final. Session restore is harness work outside the model.

nodes.label: append_messages
nodes.detail: At the end of a turn, the harness writes assistant/tool_result/final messages back to JSONL. The next -c sees the same ledger.

diagrams.label: System prompt layers
diagrams.title: AGENT.md and MEMORY.md do not enter messages
diagrams.summary: Day 6 puts project rules and the memory index into the system prompt. The messages list stores conversation history; compact only touches messages.

nodes.label: Core prompt
nodes.detail: The core system prompt describes the Agent's baseline behavior: read/write files, run commands, search, and ask the user.

nodes.label: AGENT.md
nodes.detail: Project rules are handwritten by the developer. At startup, cwd/AGENT.md is wrapped in a <project-rules> block.

nodes.label: MEMORY.md index
nodes.detail: The long-term memory index comes from .agent/memory/MEMORY.md. It stores short hooks and links; full bodies stay behind memory_recall.

nodes.label: build_system_prompt
nodes.detail: This function runs once at CLI cold start. Each layer is optional; missing files are skipped. The joined text is passed through system=.

nodes.label: Provider.complete
nodes.detail: AnthropicProvider sends the system prompt as a top-level request field, sibling to messages. It is not messages[0].

nodes.label: compact(messages)
nodes.detail: compact only compresses messages. AGENT.md and MEMORY.md travel through system=, so they are not compressed away.

diagrams.label: Long-term memory
diagrams.title: How memdir remembers facts across sessions
diagrams.summary: The model can only propose memory_write / memory_recall tool_use calls. The harness tool runtime writes topic files, updates the index, and scans memories.

nodes.label: Model
nodes.detail: The model notices a fact worth saving and proposes a memory_write call. It does not write files directly.

nodes.label: Permission gate
nodes.detail: memory_recall is readonly. memory_write is a low-risk write: default/acceptEdits allow it, while plan mode still denies it.

nodes.label: memory_write
nodes.detail: write_memory writes both .agent/memory/<type>/<slug>.md and one MEMORY.md index row, so the model cannot forget the second step.

nodes.label: topic file
nodes.detail: The topic file stores the full memory body. Fully Chinese titles fall back to a stable mem-<hash>.md filename via sha1.

nodes.label: MEMORY.md
nodes.detail: The index stores only a short hook. It enters the system prompt at startup; the model can call memory_recall when it needs the full body.

nodes.label: Fresh session
nodes.detail: A fresh session has no old messages, but build_system_prompt reads MEMORY.md, so the model still sees the long-term memory index.

nodes.label: memory_recall
nodes.detail: recall_memory scans topic files across the four type directories; it does not depend on the index. If a topic file was written but the index failed, recall can still find it.
